front: Play: two characters wait by a lone tree on a country road; they discuss hanging themselves; a few leaves appear between acts despite one day passing
Play: Lucky delivers a rambling monologue that stops only when his bowler hat is removed
Play: the author said a Caspar David Friedrich painting of two men contemplating the moon 'was the source' of this play
Play: Nagg and Nell, who lost their legs in a bicycling accident in the Ardennes, live in ashbins; their blind son Hamm abuses servant Clov who cannot sit
Play: set shaped like a human skull; title is a chess allusion; Nell says 'nothing is funnier than unhappiness'
Novel: detective Jacques Moran pursues a title character who travels on a bicycle searching for his mother
Novel: ends 'You must go on. I can't go on. I'll go on.'; features a character named Mahood who is legless, armless, and speechless
Novel: protagonist befriends prostitute Celia, plays chess with schizophrenic Mr. Endon, and burns to death when gas is turned on instead of toilet flushed
Play: Winnie is buried progressively deeper in earth; her parasol catches fire; she plays a music box waltz from The Merry Widow
Play: a one-man show in which the title character listens to recordings of his past self on his birthday; eats bananas; covers face with 'old stancher'
Play: spotlight focused only on a woman's mouth eight feet above the stage in pitch darkness; a black-robed Auditor makes 'gestures of helpless compassion'
Author: influenced by the philosopher Arnold Geulincx; first published work was a Schopenhauer-influenced monograph on Proust
Author: stabbed by a pimp named Prudent; James Joyce paid for his hospital room
Author: wrote the novel Watt while hiding from the Gestapo; set in Mr. Knott's house
Author: 'Fail again. Fail better.' from this author's prose piece Worstward Ho
